title: Contradictions in the Bible
subtitle: Commonly cited narrative tensions, why they matter, and the main responses
summary: This draft examines commonly cited Bible contradictions without confusing them with textual variants or mere differences of emphasis. Each example identifies the competing claims, explains why critics call them contradictory, and notes the principal harmonizing and historical-critical responses.

sections.title: What This Page Does—and Does Not—Call a Contradiction
sections.body:
This is one ordered catalogue, from Genesis to the New Testament. A contradiction is more than a difference in emphasis: it is a case where two claims appear unable to be true in the same respect. Every entry gives the passages, why critics call them contradictory, and the main response offered by Christian interpreters.

The labels are careful. Some examples are readily harmonized; some may reflect selective narration, paraphrase, or a copying issue; and some remain substantial tensions. This page does not call manuscript additions or wording variants contradictions. Those ask which wording is earliest, and belong in the separate textual-variants study.

sections.title: 1. Genesis: Creation, the Flood, and the Divine Name
sections.body:
**Creation sequence — Genesis 1:11-27 / Genesis 2:5-22.** Genesis 1 reads from plants to animals to humanity; Genesis 2 can read as man, garden, animals, then woman. Critics call the sequence incompatible. The usual response is that Genesis 2 zooms in on humanity rather than supplying a second day-by-day order; historical-critical readers often see two distinct creation traditions.

**Creation of woman — Genesis 1:27 / Genesis 2:21-22.** One passage presents male and female together, the other narrates man before woman. The response is again that the two chapters have different literary aims; the criticism is strongest if both are required to be complete chronological reports.

**Animals in the ark — Genesis 6:19-20 / Genesis 7:2-3.** One instruction says two of every kind; the later instruction says seven pairs of clean animals and birds, two of unclean animals. Critics call the totals inconsistent. The standard harmony reads two as the general minimum and the extra clean animals as an exception.

**The divine name — Genesis 4:26 / Genesis 12:8 / Exodus 6:3.** Genesis uses YHWH before Moses, while Exodus says the patriarchs did not know God by that name. Defenders distinguish knowing the word from receiving its covenant meaning; critics see a conflict about historical knowledge of the name.

sections.title: 2. Torah: God, Responsibility, and Narrative Details
sections.body:
**Seeing God — Genesis 32:30 / Exodus 24:9-11 / Exodus 33:20 / John 1:18.** Jacob and the Sinai elders are said to see God, while Exodus and John say no one can see God. The common response distinguishes a visible manifestation from God's full, unmediated essence. Critics reply that the wording still creates a serious tension.

**Does God change his mind? — Genesis 6:6 / Exodus 32:14 / 1 Samuel 15:11 / Numbers 23:19 / 1 Samuel 15:29.** Several texts say God regretted or relented, while others deny that God changes his mind. Defenders distinguish a change in action toward people from a change in character or ultimate purpose; critics see both affirmation and denial of change.

**Children and parents' sin — Exodus 20:5 / Deuteronomy 24:16 / Ezekiel 18:20.** Exodus speaks of visiting iniquity on later generations; Deuteronomy and Ezekiel reject punishing children for a parent's sin. The response distinguishes social consequences from individual legal guilt.

**Hardening and testing — Exodus 7:3 / Exodus 8:15 / Genesis 22:1 / James 1:13.** Pharaoh sometimes hardens his own heart and sometimes God hardens it; God tests Abraham while James says God tempts no one. Defenders distinguish ultimate sovereignty from immediate choice, and testing from an enticement to evil. Critics ask whether those distinctions are present in each story.

**Moses' father-in-law and the move to Egypt — Exodus 2:18 / Exodus 3:1 / Genesis 46:27 / Acts 7:14.** Moses' father-in-law is called Reuel and Jethro; Genesis counts seventy members entering Egypt while Acts says seventy-five. Multiple names and the Septuagint-based total are common responses, but the received wordings differ.

**Jacob's burial and the Shechem purchase — Genesis 50:13 / Acts 7:15-16.** Genesis says Jacob was buried at Machpelah, purchased by Abraham; Acts connects the burial with Shechem and a purchase by Jacob. Harmonizers propose that Acts compresses several family burials; critics see conflated locations and purchasers.

sections.title: 3. Samuel, Kings, and Chronicles: Parallel-Account Tensions
sections.body:
**Who incited David's census? — 2 Samuel 24:1 / 1 Chronicles 21:1.** Samuel names the LORD; Chronicles names Satan. The main harmonization distinguishes ultimate permission from immediate agency. Historical-critical readers often see Chronicles revising Samuel's direct attribution to God.

**Census totals — 2 Samuel 24:9 / 1 Chronicles 21:5.** Israel and Judah receive different totals in the two accounts. Different counting methods or a scribal number error are proposed; critics say one historical census cannot have both totals.

**Horses, stalls, and purchase price — 2 Samuel 8:4 / 1 Chronicles 18:4; 1 Kings 4:26 / 2 Chronicles 9:25; 2 Samuel 24:24 / 1 Chronicles 21:25.** Parallel reports give differing numbers for captured horsemen, Solomon's stalls, and David's payment for the threshing floor. These are among the clearest numerical tensions; copying, scope, and unit differences are proposed but not stated by the texts.

**Royal ages and family details — 2 Kings 8:26 / 2 Chronicles 22:2; 2 Kings 24:8 / 2 Chronicles 36:9; 2 Samuel 6:23 / 2 Samuel 21:8.** Ahaziah and Jehoiachin are given different ages, and Michal is said to have no child yet later appears connected with five sons. Scribal error and kinship terminology are common replies.

**Saul, Goliath, and the sacred bread — 1 Samuel 31:4-5 / 2 Samuel 1:8-10; 2 Samuel 21:19 / 1 Chronicles 20:5; 1 Samuel 21:1 / Mark 2:26.** Saul dies by his own sword in Samuel but an Amalekite claims to have killed him; one text names Elhanan as Goliath's killer while Chronicles calls the victim Goliath's brother; Mark names Abiathar where Samuel names Ahimelech. Defenders invoke a false Amalekite, a scribal omission, and a broader priestly-era expression.

**The famine length — 2 Samuel 24:13 / 1 Chronicles 21:12.** Samuel's traditional wording gives seven years and Chronicles gives three. Some ancient witnesses read three in Samuel, making this especially likely to involve textual transmission rather than two deliberate historical claims.

sections.title: 4. The Gospels: Origins, Ministry, and Chronology
sections.body:
**Jesus' genealogies — Matthew 1:16 / Luke 3:23; Matthew 1:6 / Luke 3:31.** Matthew calls Joseph son of Jacob and traces David's line through Solomon; Luke calls him son of Heli and traces through Nathan. Matthew also presents three sets of fourteen generations in Matthew 1:17. Defenders propose legal versus biological lines, levirate marriage, or an omitted maternal wording; critics note none is stated explicitly.

**Infancy setting and date — Matthew 2:13-23 / Luke 2:22-39; Luke 1:5 / Luke 2:1-2.** Matthew includes flight to Egypt after Bethlehem; Luke moves from the temple presentation back to Nazareth without that episode. Luke also connects Jesus' birth with both Herod and Quirinius's census. A harmony inserts an unmentioned interval or proposes a different reading of Luke's census wording; critics see distinct infancy traditions and a chronological difficulty.

**Who asked, how many were healed, and where? — Matthew 8:5 / Luke 7:3; Matthew 9:18 / Mark 5:23; Matthew 8:28 / Mark 5:2; Matthew 20:29 / Luke 18:35.** The centurion comes personally or sends elders; Jairus says his daughter is dead or dying; Matthew has two demoniacs where Mark has one; Luke's blind-man healing is while approaching Jericho, Matthew's while leaving. Representative speech, selective naming, and multiple events are proposed.

**Mission instructions and public acts — Matthew 10:9-10 / Mark 6:8-9; John 2:13-16 / Mark 11:15-17; Matthew 21:19 / Mark 11:20.** Mark permits a staff and sandals where Matthew forbids them; John puts the temple cleansing near the start, Synoptics in the final week; Matthew's fig tree withers at once, Mark's disciples notice later. Defenders appeal to wording distinctions, two cleansings, and compressed narration.

**The last supper and crucifixion time — Mark 14:12 / John 18:28 / John 19:14; Mark 15:25 / John 19:14.** Mark naturally presents the meal as Passover and the crucifixion at the third hour; John still has officials preparing to eat Passover and places the trial around the sixth hour. Different calendars, broader use of Passover, and different systems of timekeeping are the principal proposals. This is one of the most discussed Gospel chronology tensions.

**Passion details — Matthew 26:34 / Mark 14:30; Matthew 27:32 / John 19:17; Mark 15:23 / John 19:29; Matthew 27:37 / Mark 15:26 / Luke 23:38 / John 19:19; Matthew 27:44 / Luke 23:39-43; Matthew 27:46 / Luke 23:46 / John 19:30; Mark 15:38-39 / Luke 23:45-47.** The accounts vary over the rooster warning, who carried the cross, the drink, the inscription, the criminals' response, the curtain and centurion sequence, and Jesus' last words. Harmonizers allow successive actions and paraphrased sayings; critics argue that the accumulation makes a single exact chronology difficult.

sections.title: 5. Judas and the Resurrection Accounts
sections.body:
**Judas's death and field — Matthew 27:3-10 / Acts 1:18-19.** Matthew says Judas hanged himself and priests bought the field with returned money; Acts says Judas obtained a field and fell headlong, bursting open. Matthew links the name to blood money, Acts to Judas's death. A combined sequence is possible, but neither author narrates it; critics see two traditions.

**The prophecy attribution — Matthew 27:9-10 / Zechariah 11:12-13 / Jeremiah 32:6-9.** Matthew attributes a silver-and-potter-field citation to Jeremiah, while the closest wording is in Zechariah. Defenders propose a composite citation or Jeremiah as a heading; critics see an attribution problem.

**Who visited the tomb? — Matthew 28:1 / Mark 16:1 / Luke 24:10 / John 20:1.** The Gospels name different women, and John initially names Mary Magdalene alone. One account may name only the best-known visitor, but critics note that the lists and sequences are not identical.

**Stone and messengers — Matthew 28:2-5 / Mark 16:4-5 / Luke 24:2-4 / John 20:1 / John 20:12.** Matthew narrates an angel rolling the stone; others find it already moved. Matthew and Mark mention one messenger, Luke and John two. The usual response is selective reporting, not a claim that only one was present.

**Did the women report it? — Mark 16:8 / Matthew 28:8 / Luke 24:9.** Mark's earliest ending says they told no one because they were afraid; Matthew and Luke say they reported it. Defenders take Mark's silence as temporary, while critics read its ending as a divergent conclusion.

**Galilee, Jerusalem, and the ascension — Matthew 28:7 / Luke 24:49 / Luke 24:51 / Acts 1:3.** Matthew directs the disciples to Galilee; Luke emphasizes remaining near Jerusalem. Luke's Gospel can read as a same-day ascension, Acts specifies forty days. A harmonization supplies intervening events; critics see different narrative frameworks.

**Elijah and ascent — 2 Kings 2:11 / John 3:13.** Elijah is taken into heaven, while John says no one has ascended into heaven except the Son of Man. Defenders distinguish being taken by God from ascending by one's own authority.

sections.title: 6. Acts and the Epistles: Retellings and Doctrinal Tensions
sections.body:
**Paul's conversion — Acts 9:7 / Acts 22:9 / Acts 26:14-18.** Acts varies over what Paul's companions heard or saw and how much of the commission Jesus gave there. Defenders distinguish hearing a sound from understanding speech and treat later accounts as summaries. Critics see speeches shaped for different audiences.

**Faith, works, and judgment — Romans 3:28 / James 2:24 / Romans 2:6 / Matthew 25:31-46.** Paul says a person is justified apart from works of law; James says a person is justified by works and not faith alone; other texts picture judgment according to deeds. Christian theology distinguishes works of law, living faith, initial justification, and evidence at final judgment. Critics question whether the distinctions resolve the plain contrast.

**Law, burdens, and sin — Matthew 5:18 / Ephesians 2:15 / Hebrews 8:13; Galatians 6:2 / Galatians 6:5; 1 John 1:8 / 1 John 3:9.** The Law is enduring yet abolished or obsolete; believers carry one another's burdens yet each carries their own; believers have sin yet those born of God do not sin. Defenders appeal to covenantal context, different Greek terms for burdens, and habitual rather than every act of sin.

**Women, circumcision, peace, family, and righteousness — 1 Corinthians 11:5 / 1 Corinthians 14:34; Genesis 17:13 / Galatians 5:2; John 14:27 / Matthew 10:34; Exodus 20:12 / Luke 14:26; Romans 3:10 / Genesis 6:9 / Job 1:1 / Luke 1:6.** These pairs are often cited because they appear to reverse one another: women pray and prophesy yet are silent, circumcision is everlasting yet warned against, Jesus gives peace yet a sword, parents are honoured yet family is described with a hate-family command, and nobody is righteous yet several people are called righteous. Standard responses use local setting, covenant boundary, hyperbole, and different senses of righteousness.

sections.title: How to Weigh the Examples
sections.body:
The strongest examples are usually those where parallel accounts assign different numbers, names, causes, dates, or direct quotations to the same event: the census agent, David's totals, Judas's death, the genealogies, and Passover chronology. Differences in a selective list are weaker unless a passage claims completeness.

A possible harmony is not the same as a demonstrated harmony. The fair test is whether it follows from the passages' normal wording and context, or requires unstated events and special definitions. The opposite mistake is also common: different ancient accounts are not automatically contradictions merely because they choose different details.

sections.title: What Is Kept Separate: Textual Variants
sections.body: The longer ending of Mark, the woman caught in adultery, the Comma Johanneum, and KJV-only verses are important manuscript questions: Mark 16:9-20, John 7:53-8:11, and 1 John 5:7 are familiar examples. They are not counted here as contradictions. The question is whether a later copy contains the earliest wording, not whether two assertions in the same text can both be true. See the textual-variants article for manuscript evidence and translation notes.

sections.title: A Fair Conclusion
sections.body:
This catalogue now includes the major examples from the material you sent, in biblical order, with direct references beside each claim. It does not turn every difference into a contradiction, and it does not hide differences that require a proposed harmony.

The most responsible conclusion is specific: some tensions are easy to explain, some are likely connected with textual transmission, and others remain debated because the accounts make different claims about the same event. Readers should compare the full passages before deciding whether any proposed resolution is convincing.

bibleVerses.text: God created man in his own image. In God’s image he created him; male and female he created them.

Yahweh God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep. As the man slept, he took one of his ribs, and closed up the flesh in its place.
Yahweh God made a woman from the rib which he had taken from the man, and brought her to the man.

Of every living thing of all flesh, you shall bring two of every sort into the ship, to keep them alive with you. They shall be male and female.
Of the birds after their kind, of the livestock after their kind, of every creeping thing of the ground after its kind, two of every sort will come to you, to keep them alive.

You shall take seven pairs of every clean animal with you, the male and his female. Of the animals that are not clean, take two, the male and his female.
Also of the birds of the sky, seven and seven, male and female, to keep seed alive on the surface of all the earth.
Again Yahweh’s anger burned against Israel, and he moved David against them, saying, “Go, count Israel and Judah.”
Satan stood up against Israel, and moved David to take a census of Israel.
Jacob became the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, from whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.
Jesus himself, when he began to teach, was about thirty years old, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli,

Then Judas, who betrayed him, when he saw that Jesus was condemned, felt remorse, and brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders, saying, “I have sinned in that I betrayed innocent blood.” But they said, “What is that to us? You see to it.” He threw down the pieces of silver in the sanctuary, and departed. He went away and hanged himself.

Now this man obtained a field with the reward for his wickedness, and falling headlong, his body burst open, and all his intestines gushed out. It became known to everyone who lived in Jerusalem that in their language that field was called ‘Akeldama,’ that is, ‘The field of blood.’
On the first day of unleavened bread, when they sacrificed the Passover, his disciples asked him, “Where do you want us to go and prepare that you may eat the Passover?”
They led Jesus therefore from Caiaphas into the Praetorium. It was early, and they themselves didn’t enter into the Praetorium, that they might not be defiled, but might eat the Passover.
Now it was the Preparation Day of the Passover, at about the sixth hour. He said to the Jews, “Behold, your King!”
It was the third hour, and they crucified him.
Now after the Sabbath, as it began to dawn on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary came to see the tomb.
When the Sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James, and Salome, bought spices, that they might come and anoint him.
Now they were Mary Magdalene, Joanna, and Mary the mother of James. The other women with them told these things to the apostles.
Now on the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene went early, while it was still dark, to the tomb, and saw the stone taken away from the tomb.

furtherReading.title: Jesus, Interrupted: Revealing the Hidden Contradictions in the Bible
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: A readable historical-critical presentation of major tensions in the Bible; readers should also consult scholarly responses and primary texts.

furtherReading.title: The Historical Reliability of the Gospels
furtherReading.author: Craig L. Blomberg
furtherReading.note: An evangelical defense of Gospel reliability that presents harmonizations of alleged discrepancies.

furtherReading.title: The Bible Unearthed
furtherReading.author: Israel Finkelstein and Neil Asher Silberman
furtherReading.note: A widely discussed archaeological and historical-critical study of biblical narratives; its conclusions remain debated among scholars.
